title: Writing Insurance-Defensible Restoration Scopes — Line Items, IICRC Justification & Unit Rates
description: How to write water damage restoration scopes that hold up to insurance adjuster scrutiny — structuring line items, citing IICRC S500 for justification, unit rate benchmarks, and avoiding the most common scope-writing mistakes in Australia.
author: Phill McGurk | RestoreAssist
transcript:
## What Makes a Scope Insurance-Defensible?

An insurance-defensible scope is one that:
1. Accurately describes the work performed or proposed
2. Justifies each item with reference to applicable standards, observed damage, or documented measurements
3. Uses unit rates that can be verified against industry benchmarks
4. Presents information in a structured format that an adjuster can audit efficiently

A scope that meets these criteria will typically be reviewed, approved, and paid without extended back-and-forth. A scope that does not will generate queries, reductions, and disputes — all of which cost time and damage the client relationship.

## Structuring Line Items

Each line item in a restoration scope should contain:

- **Work description**: Clear, specific description of the work (e.g., 'Remove and dispose of wet carpet and underlay, bedroom 2, 12 m²' — not 'Floor removal')
- **Quantity**: The measured quantity with the unit of measure (m², LM, each, hours)
- **Unit rate**: The cost per unit of measure
- **Total**: Quantity × rate
- **Justification note**: A brief reference to why this item is included (e.g., 'Carpet and underlay wet — Category 1 loss exceeding 48 hours, escalated per IICRC S500:2025 §7.1' or 'Required under IICRC S500:2025 Appendix B for Class 2, 60 m² affected area')

The justification note is the most commonly omitted element and the one that creates the most dispute. Without it, the adjuster must either accept the line item on trust or request clarification. With it, the standard has already answered their question.

## Citing IICRC S500 Effectively

References to IICRC S500 in a scope should be specific. 'Per IICRC S500' without a section number is less convincing than 'Per IICRC S500:2025 §7.3 — porous materials contacted by Category 3 water require removal'. Specific citations demonstrate technical knowledge and make it clear the scope is not padding.

Key sections to cite by scope item type:

- **Porous material removal (Category 3)**: §7.3
- **Equipment quantities**: Appendix B
- **Antimicrobial treatment (Category 2/3)**: §7.6
- **Drying documentation requirements**: §9.1, §11.4
- **Wall cavity drying (Class 3)**: §9.3

## Unit Rate Benchmarks in Australia

Australian restoration unit rates are benchmarked against several sources:

- **Xactimate pricing database** (US-origin, used by many Australian insurers as a reference)
- **ABRS (Australian Building Restoration Services) rate schedule**
- **State-based construction cost guides** (Rawlinsons, Cordell) for reconstruction items

Equipment daily hire rates should reflect actual market rates. Justifiably high rates (e.g., for specialist equipment like desiccant dehumidifiers or negative air machines) should be documented with hire cost references or market quotes.

For labour rates, the applicable Modern Award (Cleaning Services Award or Building and Construction General On-site Award, depending on the nature of work) establishes minimum rates. Specialist restoration technicians typically command above-award rates — justify these by referencing relevant certifications (IICRC WRT, ASD, FSRT, etc.).

## Common Scope-Writing Mistakes

- Generic line item descriptions that cannot be matched to specific damage items
- Equipment quantities that exceed IICRC S500 ratios without explanation
- Reconstruction line items mixed into the restoration scope without a clear boundary
- Scope submitted before moisture map is complete (areas of damage are uncertain)
- Provisional sums without a basis for the estimate (inspections that did not access affected cavities)
- No drying completion documentation attached at final invoice stage
videoScript:
G'day, welcome to RestoreAssist. Today we're going through scope writing — how to structure a water damage restoration scope that gets paid without a fight.

I've seen a lot of scopes over the years, and the ones that get queried or reduced almost always have the same problems: vague line item descriptions, no justification for why the work is included, and equipment quantities that don't link back to documented measurements. Let's fix all of that.

First, line item structure. Every line item needs four things: a clear description of the specific work in that specific location, the measured quantity with the unit of measure, the unit rate, and a justification note.

That last one — the justification note — is what separates a scope that gets approved from one that generates a query. The note should answer the adjuster's obvious question: why is this item in the scope? 'Remove and dispose of wet carpet and underlay, bedroom 2, 12 m², carpet wet at Category 1 loss >48 hours, per IICRC S500:2025 §7.1.' The standard answers the question before it's asked.

For IICRC citations, be specific. Not just 'per IICRC S500' — include the section number. Category 3 porous material removal: cite §7.3. Equipment quantities: cite Appendix B with the damage class and affected area. Antimicrobial treatment: cite §7.6. Specific citations demonstrate that you know the standard and that the scope is technically driven, not speculative.

On unit rates: Australian insurers reference Xactimate, ABRS rate schedules, or state-based construction cost guides like Rawlinsons for benchmarking. Rates that fall within those ranges will be accepted without question. Rates outside the range need supporting documentation — either a hire cost reference for specialised equipment or a market rate justification.

The most common mistakes I see: generic descriptions that can't be matched to specific damage items, equipment quantities that exceed IICRC ratios with no explanation, and scopes submitted before the moisture map is complete. That last one is a big one — if your moisture map isn't done, your affected area is uncertain, and the scope will be challenged.

RestoreAssist generates scope line items from your inspection data — moisture map readings, damage class, affected area — and pre-populates the IICRC section references automatically. The scope is built from the measurement data, not from memory or template.

Head to restoreassist.com.au to see it in action.
